title: Bank Statement Analysis for Small Manufacturers
meta: Manufacturers carry long cash-conversion cycles, large material buys, and B2B receivables. Learn how lenders analyze manufacturing bank statements for equipment and working capital.
content:
Manufacturing Has the Longest Cash Cycle in Small Business

Small manufacturers buy raw materials, convert them over weeks, ship to B2B customers, then wait 30-90 days to get paid. That long cash-conversion cycle dominates the bank statement and is the central thing a lender must understand.

How Manufacturing Revenue Appears

  • Customer ACH and check deposits: Fewer but larger B2B payments, often on net-30/60 terms.
  • Raw-material outflows: Large purchases ahead of production runs.
  • Payroll: Consistent and significant — production labor is a major recurring outflow.
  • Equipment-finance payments: Machinery loans and leases as normal capital deployment.

The Working-Capital Gap

Because materials and labor are paid long before customers pay, growing manufacturers are chronically short on working capital — paradoxically, faster growth widens the gap. Lenders should read large material outflows followed by delayed customer deposits as the normal rhythm of the business, not distress.

What Lenders Focus On

  • Customer payment timing and concentration (reliance on one buyer is a risk)
  • Gross margin implied by material outflows vs. sales deposits
  • Payroll consistency and equipment obligations
  • NSF events tied to the cash-conversion gap

Bottom Line

Manufacturers are strong candidates for working capital, equipment financing, and lines of credit that bridge the cash-conversion cycle. StatementScrub surfaces customer concentration and the timing gap that define manufacturing risk.

Related reading: Wholesale distributor analysis | Understanding cash flow ratios | Business line of credit analysis
